segments.title: Trading older capital ships for new construction
segments.summary: The counterfactual begins by asking what the Royal Navy might receive if it surrendered more 13.5-inch ships, including HMS Tiger, under the Washington settlement.
segments.body: The discussion treats a larger British new-construction allowance as a bargain rather than a free gift: older ships disappear from the fleet in exchange. The possible 525,000- and 558,950-ton totals lead to five or six 35,000-ton ships, but they also reduce the number of hulls Britain can keep on station. That trade-off frames the whole design question.

evidence.note: The transcript sets out exchanging 13.5-inch ships for five or six new ships under alternative tonnage totals.

segments.title: Why a capability needs more than one hull
segments.summary: A simple availability rule explains why the Royal Navy would value several fast capital ships rather than a single exceptional one.
segments.body: One ship represents a capability only when it happens to be available, while two provide a usual but fragile capability. Three create continuous availability, and larger numbers add presence and surge capacity. The point turns a paper comparison of individual designs into a question about a fleet that can absorb maintenance, damage, and deployment demands.

evidence.note: The speaker lays out the progression from one available capability to continuous availability and surge capacity.

segments.title: A 28-knot alternative to Nelson and Rodney
segments.summary: The proposed alternative is a 35,000-ton, 16-inch-gun, 28-knot capital ship rather than the heavily protected two-ship Nelson design.
segments.body: The design logic starts with the loss of HMS Tiger and the need to retain a fast element in the fleet. A 28-knot ship is presented as a G3-influenced compromise between a conventional battleship and a battlecruiser. The simulation uses this hypothetical class to explore what modestly reduced protection could buy when a navy can procure several ships instead of two.

evidence.note: The transcript proposes 35,000 tons, 16-inch guns, and 28 knots, then introduces the simulated design.

segments.title: Quantity versus armour in the battle line
segments.summary: More 28-knot ships could make a powerful fast battle line even if each vessel accepted less armour than Nelson and Rodney.
segments.body: With only two ships, the speaker would prioritize armour because each loss is strategically severe. With four, five, or six, he argues that a common 28-knot class becomes more attractive than an exceptionally protected pair. Modernized Queen Elizabeth-class ships could then join the same fast battle line, making speed and numbers mutually reinforcing.

evidence.note: The speaker contrasts armour for a two-ship force with a 28-knot class of four to six ships.

segments.title: Testing the design against Bismarck
segments.summary: A simulated Denmark Strait-style action illustrates how two 16-inch ships can overwhelm a faster opponent despite lighter protection.
segments.body: The game scenario is not offered as a historical prediction, but as a way to visualize the design trade. The hypothetical ships retain a substantial belt while yielding some protection for speed and a more numerous force. The engagement emphasizes concentration of guns and the difficulty of escaping a fast battle line when sustained high speed reduces operational radius.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses reduced armour, the simulated Bismarck opponent, and the effect of double the number of heavy guns.

segments.title: Procurement is about a fleet, not one ship
segments.summary: The central lesson is that a navy buys a capability across a fleet, balancing exceptionally protected ships against the resilience of larger numbers.
segments.body: Nelson and Rodney are described as formidable anchors of a battle line, but a pair must be treated as irreplaceable assets. A larger class can accept a different balance between protection, performance, and loss tolerance. The comparison is extended to contemporary forces: larger, more expensive platforms may carry more capability, while smaller platforms provide presence in more places.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts two heavily protected ships with a larger class and explains fleet-level procurement choices.

segments.title: The machinery route to 28 knots
segments.summary: Reduced armour and a rearranged machinery plant are presented as the technical route from Nelson and Rodney’s 45,000 shp to a faster design.
segments.body: The original ships’ two-shaft machinery is contrasted with a proposed three-shaft arrangement using more boilers and geared turbines. This is a design sketch rather than a claim that the historical ships could be remade without compromise. Its purpose is to show why the speed choice becomes plausible once the navy is ordering a class rather than preserving two singular vessels.

evidence.note: The speaker compares the historical machinery with a three-shaft, higher-power hypothetical arrangement.

segments.title: How a larger order could sustain industry
segments.summary: Building six Nelson-type ships in the 1920s would keep shipyards, armour manufacture, and their supply chains more active.
segments.body: The discussion identifies a second-order effect often hidden by a ship-to-ship comparison: capital-ship orders sustain industrial skills and capacity. Depression-era pressures would still exist, but treaty-authorized construction could prevent these orders from being cancelled. The argument links fleet policy to the industrial base required to support later naval power.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies shipyards, armour production, and supply chains as effects of a six-ship programme.

segments.title: A fast battle line would reshape rival designs
segments.summary: A Royal Navy battle line of 28-knot ships would force Germany and the United States to reconsider the speed, displacement, and protection of their own capital ships.
segments.body: A 30-knot German battleship may no longer have enough margin to evade a large British force that can sustain 28 knots. The resulting pressure would affect decisions about machinery, armour, displacement, and operational roles. The speaker also points to consequences for British carriers, which would need to operate with a faster battle line.

evidence.note: The transcript connects a 28-knot Royal Navy line to German, American, and carrier-design consequences.

segments.title: Five ships as the conservative treaty outcome
segments.summary: Five 35,000-ton ships are presented as the cautious estimate, enough to form a fast line alongside modernized Queen Elizabeth-class battleships.
segments.body: The speaker returns to the treaty arithmetic and treats five ships as the more conservative result. Combined with five Queen Elizabeths modernized to 28 knots, five R-class ships, and the battlecruisers, this would transform British capital-ship force structure. The sacrifice is that the older R-class would receive less modernization attention and become the next replacement priority.

evidence.note: The speaker identifies five ships as conservative and describes the resulting 28-knot British battle line.

segments.title: Modernizing the Queen Elizabeth class for a common speed
segments.summary: A 28-knot Nelson class would give the Royal Navy a stronger reason to modernize the Queen Elizabeth class to match its battle-line speed.
segments.body: The Warspite reconstruction is used to show that additional boilers and altered internal arrangements could support higher speed. Historically, the fleet did not have the same incentive because the R-class and the two slower Nelsons set a different tempo. In this scenario, common speed makes the modernization of the Queen Elizabeths a fleet-level priority rather than an isolated technical possibility.

evidence.note: The transcript uses Warspite's boiler arrangements to argue that Queen Elizabeths could be raised to 28 knots.

segments.title: Deployment options for a faster British battle line
segments.summary: A larger fast capital-ship force opens different choices for Mediterranean, Far Eastern, and Atlantic deployment.
segments.body: With five or six ships rather than two, the Nelson squadron need not remain concentrated at home as a uniquely scarce asset. The discussion suggests a Mediterranean role, Queen Elizabeths as reinforcement for the Far East, and R-class ships in the Atlantic as later reinforcements. The larger lesson is that numbers and common speed create strategic flexibility before a battle is ever fought.

evidence.note: The transcript sketches Mediterranean, Far East, and Atlantic roles for the alternative battle squadrons.
